retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The first person to encounter the patient is the triage nurse, who must be familiar with the symptoms of the disorder. Time is of the essence, and the nurse should be aware of torsion, quickly admit the patient, and quickly notify the ED physician. The ED physician should consult with a radiologist for the appropriate test and, at the same time, consult with the urologist. The nurses should prepare the patient as if he will be going for surgery by keeping the child NPO and having all the blood work completed.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound is not a perfect test for testicular torsion, especially in the very young. For example, 40% of neonatal testicles may have no apparent color flow Doppler. If the clinical concern is high, seek urologic surgery consultation immediately. Any delay in treatment could result in testicular necrosis and loss. The typical window of opportunity for surgical intervention and testicular salvage is 6 hours from the onset of pain. Therefore, early urologic surgery consultation upon presentation may be critical even in the absence of confirmatory testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency that almost always presents to the emergency department. The disorder is usually managed by an interprofessional team.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In neonates, bilateral scrotal exploration is done. Contralateral orchiopexy is always done to prevent future torsion. Patients who require an orchiectomy for a non-viable testis usually have a testicular prosthesis inserted. The prosthesis is usually inserted 4-6 months after the initial surgery to allow for the inflammation to subside

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the epidemiology of testicular torsion. Outline the presenting signs and symptoms of testicular torsion. Summarize considerations that influence the management of testicular torsion. Explain how the facilitation of interprofessional team education and discussion can optimize the effective detection of testicular torsion and inform the need for subsequent evaluations. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Manual detorsion should be attempted if urological intervention is not immediately available. The abnormal testicle should be rotated in a medial to lateral direction (open book) 180 degrees and then evaluated for pain relief. If the pain is increased, consider rotating the testicle in the opposite direction. Ultrasound can also be used serially to evaluate for return of blood flow at the bedside. If unsuccessful, further manual detorsion may be attempted as the testicle can twist 180 degrees. [4] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Clinical Significance -- Testicular Torsion
retrieved_docs.content: Testicular torsion is diagnosable using a color doppler ultrasound; this is the gold standard for the evaluation of the acute scrotum. [174] Testicular torsion management involves emergency surgical exploration. Following this, the testis is untwisted and wrapped in a warm soaked gauze. The testis is then observed to see if there is an improvement in the color of the testis. To reduce the likelihood of the contralateral testis undergoing torsion, it is fixed along with the testis, which underwent torsion. [171]

retrieved_docs.title: Appendix Testis Torsion -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: If a testicular appendage is more than 5.6 mm on ultrasound, it should be considered suspicious for torsion of the appendage. Patients with testicular appendage torsion are likely younger than those with testicular torsion. They also will lack the "angel wing deformity" and not demonstrate the "high riding" testicle position usually associated with testicular torsion. [20] Rare causes of an acute scrotum can include incarcerated hernias and traumatic testicular ruptures. There have been reported cases of simultaneous testicular torsion with testicular appendage torsion. Removing the testicular appendages in the contralateral testis during a scrotal exploration for torsion of the testicular appendages is now considered optional but is not required. It is possible to have torsion of a testicular appendage in an undescended testicle.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The outcomes of testicular torsion depend on when the patient presents to the ED and how quickly the diagnosis is made and treatment is undertaken. Delays in diagnosis and treatment always lead to testicular atrophy. About 20% to 40% of cases of testicular torsion result in an orchiectomy. The risk of losing a testis is much higher among African Americans and younger males. For those who present within the first 6 hours of symptoms, the salvage rate is nearly 100%, but this number quickly drops to less than 50% if the delay in seeking help is more than 12 to 24 hours. More importantly, when the testis is fixed by orchiopexy, there is also a potential for future torsion. [11] [12] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Scrotal complaints are relatively common both in primary care and in the emergency department and comprise at least 0.5 percent of all emergency department visits. Testicular torsion is a true urologic emergency, and early identification is critical to prevent the need for testicular amputation. Ultrasound is the ideal imaging modality to evaluate the scrotal contents. This activity will review the most common causes of testicular torsion, the common presenting signs and symptoms, and the treatment approach according to current evidence. This activity will highlight the role of the interprofessional team in recognizing and treating testicular torsion.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Over the years, there has been a marked improvement in the salvage of the testes following torsion. However, poor results still occur, especially in African Americans, young patients, and those who lack health insurance. The best results are obtained if the surgery is done within 8 hours of symptoms. However, recurrence can also occur after orchiopexy.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: If the testing confirms torsion, the urologist is usually required to perform the surgery. The nurse should educate the family and the patient about the potential complications, including loss of the testis and infertility. The nurse should ensure that the patient is administered no food or drink by mouth and have the patient prepared to go for urgent surgery. More importantly, the nurse should avoid giving any pain medications until the patient has been seen by the urologist- or the pain medication will mask the symptoms and delay the diagnosis. Only through a systemic approach to diagnosis and treatment, is the salvage of the testis possible. [9] [10] Open communication between the team members is vital if the outcomes are to be improved. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Appendix Testis Torsion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Management of the acute scrotum should be treated as a surgical emergency until a diagnosis is confirmed. Given the situation's urgency, patients with this condition often present to the emergency department, where a triage nurse initially assesses them. The triage nurse must be aware of the potential for testicular torsion and the need for immediate action.

retrieved_docs.title: Appendix Testis Torsion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Torsion of testicular appendages is considered one of the most common causes of acute scrotal pain in prepubertal children. Therefore, it should be included in the differential for any male with an acute scrotum. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of testicular appendage torsion and explains the role of the interprofessional team in quickly diagnosing and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Scrotum Pain -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The definitive treatment of testicular torsion is surgical exploration and detorsion, usually followed by orchiopexy to prevent recurrent torsion. Manual detorsion can and often should be attempted at the bedside although its success rate is widely variable and has been reported to range from 25% to 80%. In patients with testicular torsion, the affected testicle is rotated medially. Therefore, manual detorsion should be attempted first from a medial-to-lateral direction (“opening of the book”) and may require 180 to 720 more degrees. Specifically, this requires counterclockwise rotation of the patient's right testicle or clockwise rotation of the patient's left testicle, similar to the action of opening the spine of a book. Manual detorsion is contraindicated if pain and/or suspected torsion have been present for longer than 6 hours. Point-of-care ultrasound may be useful in evaluating the direction and effectiveness of this maneuver. Successful manual detorsion should result in near-immediate relief of pain. Surgical exploration is still mandated even in the event manual detorsion is successful. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The higher the score, the greater the probability that the patient has torsion. Ultrasound is recommended for those with low scores. Those with high TWIST scores can be taken for surgery without ultrasound.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2893
retrieved_docs.content: The urogenital system (urogenitální systém) is the anatomical region implicated in both ovarian torsion (التواء المبيض) and pregnancy disorders (اضطراب الحمل). Ovarian torsion is a disease that has the urogenital system as its associated anatomic site, indicating that the torsion of the ovary occurs within this system. Likewise, pregnancy disorders are associated with the urogenital system, reflecting that complications of gestation involve structures of this region. Recognizing the urogenital system’s role in these conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate imaging, and effective management of patients presenting with ovarian torsion or pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2070
retrieved_docs.content: The urogenital system is closely linked to specific gynecologic conditions, including ovarian torsion and fallopian tube disorder. Ovarian torsion involves the twisting of the ovary, while fallopian tube disorder refers to abnormalities of the tubal structure. Both conditions are associated with the anatomic sites of the urogenital system, highlighting the importance of evaluating this system when diagnosing and managing ovarian and tubal pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: The urogenital system (urogenitální systém) is the anatomical region associated with ovarian torsion (التواء المبيض). Ovarian torsion is a gynecologic emergency in which the ovary twists around its supporting ligaments, potentially compromising vascular supply and leading to acute abdominal pain. Because the ovary is part of the female reproductive tract, this condition directly involves structures of the urogenital system. Recognizing that ovarian torsion is linked to the urogenital system is essential for timely diagnosis, appropriate imaging, and prompt surgical intervention to preserve ovarian function and prevent complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: genital as a child entity. Prostatotomy and calculus removal is identified as a type of other or therapeutic procedures; male genital. Additionally, the category other or therapeutic procedures; male genital contains the child procedure شق البروستاتا عبر الإحليل (transurethral prostate incision). Overall, the knowledge network links head‑and‑neck ear surgeries, incision techniques, middle‑ear disease management, musculoskeletal osteotomy revisions, and male genital therapeutic interventions, illustrating how specific procedural methods are shared across anatomical regions and how classification hierarchies organize related surgical specialties for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of uterus, ovarian torsion (التواء المبيض), fallopian tube disorder (اضطراب أنبوب فالوب), and glandular prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا الغدي) are all conditions that are anatomically associated with the urogenitální systém (the urogenital system). Each of these diseases involves organs that belong to the urogenital system: the uterus in cancer of uterus, the ovary in ovarian torsion, the fallopian tubes in fallopian tube disorder, and the prostate in glandular prostate cancer. Recognizing that these diverse pathologies share the same anatomic system underscores the importance of a coordinated approach to evaluation, diagnosis, and management within the urogenital tract, facilitating comprehensive patient care that addresses the interrelated nature of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1665
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopy (gi only) serves as a broad minimally invasive surgical platform that encompasses several specific gynecologic procedures. Laparoscopic lysis of adhesions is identified as a child procedure within this platform, indicating it is a specialized application of laparoscopy (gi only). Both laparoscopic salpingostomy and laparoscopy with removal of adnexal structures are classified as types of laparoscopy (gi only), confirming that they share the same operative approach. Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral is also mapped to laparoscopy (gi only), further illustrating that bilateral removal of portions of the fallopian tubes is performed using this gastrointestinal‑focused laparoscopic technique. In clinical practice, the use of laparoscopy (gi only) frequently co‑occurs with conditions such as ovarian torsion (التواء المبيض), tubo‑ovarian abscess (خراج بوقي مبيضي), and tubal rupture (فَغْرُ البُوْق). These associations suggest that such pathologies are common indications for, or complications of, the laparoscopic procedures listed, highlighting the relevance of laparoscopy (gi only) in the diagnosis and management of acute gynecologic emergencies.

Within the male genital therapeutic group, Prostatolithotomy is listed as having the child entity other or therapeutic procedures; male genital, and Prostatotomy and calculus removal is defined as an instance of other or therapeutic procedures; male genital. Additionally, the child relationship from other or therapeutic procedures; male genital to شق البروستاتا عبر الإحليل (transurethral prostate incision) further connects specific prostate surgeries to this overarching procedural class. Overall, the knowledge network links the morphological basis of cataract to its anatomical location, categorizes lens‑related disorders and their surgical management, and demonstrates how the fundamental concept of an incision is a shared methodological element across ophthalmic, urologic, and musculoskeletal therapeutic procedures.

retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 975
retrieved_docs.content: for management of ectopic pregnancy,” itself a child of removal of ectopic pregnancy. Abdominal surgeries co‑occur with الْتِهابُ الصِّفاقِ (peritonitis) and are inversely related to fallopian tube surgeries, underscoring that tubal surgeries constitute a subset of abdominal procedures. Collectively, these relationships highlight the central role of the fallopian tubes in ectopic pregnancy management, inflammatory pelvic disease, and related laparoscopic interventions, emphasizing the need for precise procedural mapping and appropriate qualifiers to optimize patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1779
retrieved_docs.content: استئصال قناة فالوب (salpingectomy) is categorized under Female Reproductive System Procedures and functions as a parent entity for several specific laparoscopic techniques. The procedures Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral; Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, unilateral; Laparoscopic salpingectomy, bilateral; and Laparoscopic salpingectomy, unilateral are all child procedures of استئصال قناة فالوب, representing bilateral or unilateral removal of the fallopian tube, either partially or completely, using minimally invasive laparoscopy. Clinically, استئصال قناة فالوب frequently co‑occurs with مرض التهاب الحوض (pelvic inflammatory disease), reflecting its common use in the management of this infection and its complications. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship of these procedures within female reproductive system interventions and their association with مرض التهاب الحوض helps clinicians select appropriate surgical approaches and anticipate related clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2834
retrieved_docs.content: Ovarian torsion (التواء المبيض) is a gynecologic disease that is anatomically associated with the urogenital system (urogenitální systém). The surgical procedure known as Cesarean hysterectomy also targets the urogenital system (urogenitální systém) as its operative field. Together, these relationships highlight the urogenital system as a common anatomical focus for both the pathology of ovarian torsion and the therapeutic intervention of Cesarean hysterectomy, underscoring its importance in the diagnosis, management, and surgical treatment of related obstetric and gynecologic conditions.

In the male genital system, transrectal prostate biopsy and open prostatectomy are children of the “diagnostic‑procedures‑male‑genital” and “therapeutic‑procedures‑male‑genital” hierarchies, respectively, and are directly associated with the prostate as the procedure site. Additional male‑genital interventions such as prostatolithotomy, prostatotomy (including calculus removal), and transurethral prostate incision (شق البروستاتا عبر الإحليل) fall under “other or therapeutic procedures; male genital,” also linked to the prostate, which is mapped to the broader “Organ” class identified as a gonadal entity. Bladder disorders (اضطراب في المثانة) include herniated urinary bladder and a composite condition “genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions,” which co‑occur with a spectrum of prostate and renal

Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs also reference the ovary as their finding site, underscoring that both inflammatory and non‑inflammatory conditions are evaluated in the same anatomical region. Collectively, the network maps ovarian and fallopian‑tube pathologies, cystic findings, and a comprehensive hierarchy of diagnostic and operative procedures to the ovary and its substructures, supporting integrated assessment and therapeutic planning in female reproductive health care.

Overall, the knowledge base connects each cancer type to its parent malignant tumor category, maps anatomical sites such as the prostate, seminal vesicles, cervix, bladder, and ovary to both disease entities and related surgical or screening procedures, and integrates these relationships within a comprehensive classification system to support patient assessment, diagnosis, and management of genitourinary and related malignancies.

retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pregnancy (حمل منتبذ) is clinically associated with laparotomy (شق البطن), because surgical entry into the abdominal cavity is frequently required for the diagnosis, evaluation, and definitive management of this rare ectopic gestation.
